reading guide service description: Each Service description in this schedule contains the same paragraphs presenting a uniform template. In this chapter each standard paragraph is explained.
standard services: This section describes the Services that are to be delivered to <klant>. The Services include any services, activities and tasks that are of a nature and type that would ordinarily be performed as an inherent subtask by an entity (internal or external) performing Services similar to the Services and/or that are generally considered to be part of the Services.
optional services: This section describes the Services that can be optionally added to the Service delivered to <klant>.
out of scope: This section describes the explicitly defined out of scope services related to the Service.
conditions: This section describes the conditions that must be met in order for Cegeka to provide the Service.

Actions in response to <klant> end-user calls and alerts originating from the Cegeka monitoring services are started. Tickets, which can be handled using standard procedures, are solved. The remaining tickets are handed over to engineering support.

Engineering support
Incidents and requests that cannot be handled by Cegeka service desk by applying standardized procedures are transferred to second line engineers. These engineers have application-specific knowledge necessary to handle these incidents with the goal of resolving them within the Service Levels as specified by their priority.
The service activity ensures the application’s technical availability is restored as per the Service Level Agreement in the event of technical failure. This doesn’t provide any guarantees on the level of functional availability and is merely allowing the functional application owner to operate and/or troubleshoot the functional application service it hosts.
In case of structural incidents, a problem ticket is created. In case an incident cannot be solved, but a work- around is available, a problem ticket is created in order to realize a structural resolution for the incident. As problems possibly lead to structural Changes in the application or infrastructure, the investigation into the problems’ cause is not in scope of this Service. This is either handled through the advanced Service or by a third party.

Application patch deployment
The service activity ensures patches are deployed onto the technical application as per the predefined patch management plan or upon explicit request of <klant>. <klant> is accountable for both timings and instructions (responsibility for instructions can reside with the product vendor). By default critical (security) patches and patch sets are included in the patch management plan.
Deployment is limited to applying the patches to the technical application as is, excluding configuration changes and activation of functionality added by the patch(es) or patch set(s). The latter is done via RFC containing content, scope and exceptions. This RFC acts as patch deployment plan.
<klant> remains in the lead by determining either the scope and high level planning or by explicitly requesting one off patch deployments unless patch planning & management is handled by Cegeka as part of the advanced service package.
By default, Cegeka performs two patch deployment rounds per year. Within such a patch deployment round, deployments are phased over the different environments (e.g. acceptance is patched before production). Systems within an environment are patched in bulk (e.g. all production systems).

Technical monitoring
Applications make use of various system resources, such as memory and CPU. These resources assigned need to be balanced in order to allow all applications on the system to work correctly. In this service, the resource assignment to a business application is measured in order to ensure that the resource utilization stays within a predefined range.
In case a business application requires a number of jobs to be executed automatically, this Service also ensures that the jobs are executed and that incidents are handled that occur if one of the jobs cannot be executed. This Service only concerns jobs that are executed by the application itself. In case the jobs are handled by a job scheduler, the job execution is part of the management of the job scheduler.

A service delivery manager is appointed for <klant>. During the service intake process of <klant>, the service delivery manager customizes the Cegeka generic IT service delivery processes and documents this in the service delivery plan. capacity management:
Capacity management is part of Cegeka’s continuous service delivery and shall ensure that IT resources are sized right to meet current and future business requirements in a cost-effective manner. It covers multiple aspects from capacity analysis to capacity planning.
Capacity analysis is concerned with:
Component capacity management
Component capacity management is embedded in Cegeka’s monitoring process. The Cegeka monitoring process provides various options for automated monitoring and alerting. If there are spikes in, for example, processing power at a particular time of the day, it shall analyse what is happening at that time and make Changes to maximize the existing IT infrastructure, for example, move a batch cycle to a quieter period.
It is implemented as follows: processing which deviates above installed thresholds, will generate an alert, which is handled via the incident management process. Recurring alerts will be handled through the problem management process for the alert analysis and for the solution, which is implemented via the Change management. This component capacity management is imbedded in the daily operational processes for <klant>.
The monitoring team implements the monitoring and alerting scheme. Alerts generate an incident, which is handled through the Incident Management process. Monitoring data is accessible and available for review (performance management). The monitoring data is retained for at least one year.
During service delivery, the technical architect keeps the monitoring plan up-to-date and the service delivery manager reviews the monitoring plan at least once a year. Specific thresholds, work instructions and process interfaces will be agreed and elaborated on during Transition.
Resource consumption is measured on infrastructure level and part of the monthly service reporting. Following capacity documents are available:
Capacity trending
Another aspect of capacity management is capacity trending. As the usage of the IT infrastructure and Services Change and functionality evolves, resource usage can Change and lead to capacity shortage if not managed. Capacity trending is based on historical data, enriched with impact from known Change (known to Cegeka) and extrapolated to the near future.
For <klant>, capacity trending shall be analysed and formally reported by Cegeka.

continuous improvement and innovation:
Continuous innovation always follows the same standard process approach, regardless of the level of focus (technical, process or governance):
Phase 1:	Establish baseline – the baseline relies upon the corner stones of operations – architecture, service performance and cost – which are kept up to date as part of the daily operations.
Phase 2: Identify opportunities – opportunities are detected throughout operations. While almost anyone in the service organisation and <klant>’s organisation can identify opportunities, it is the responsibility of our Lead Architect to keep track and prioritise them. During half-yearly workshops prepared by the Lead Architect, the topics are prepared with a clear vision in mind (see “Continuous innovation workshop”).
Phase 3: Develop capture strategy – based on the list of identified and agreed opportunities a strategy is developed to ensure how the opportunities can be “captured” and integrated in the organisation’s planning via an implementation roadmap. Smaller opportunities with a short-term return can already be initiated as an RFC via the existing change management process.
Phase 4 : Realise benefits – the roadmap provides the plan for implementation and must contain the business case on which the priority decisions have been made. Phase 4 contains the actual implementation of the opportunity at whatever level it may be – technical, procedure/process level, governance – and looks back at the initial business case(s) to ensure the benefits are realised.
Safeguarding the actual quality of the services the continuous improvement methodology is applied to a number of architecture deliverables, which have interfaces with one another and provide input to the continuous improvement roadmap. The datacenter LAN in the network for connecting and interconnecting the infrastructure.
Within the datacenter LAN, there are 2 sub layers:
The core layer is used for:
All equipment to the core is connected redundantly.
The access layer is used for:
Cegeka’s datacenter LAN design consists of a redundant Cisco Nexus 7000 series core switches.
The same type of setup is deployed in the access layer. The setup consists of 2x 5000 series Cisco Nexus switches connected to 2000 series Cisco Nexus fabric extenders (FEX). Servers in the datacenter are always redundantly connected to two different fabric extenders. These fabric extenders are connected to different Nexus 5000 parent switches.
The access switches are LACP port-channels. This ensures redundancy via the aggregation of two physical cables into one logical connection. Blade switches are configured with link state groups. In case of an uplink failure in the core switches, the link status is propagated to the server connections in the chassis.
On the server side, all network interface cards are redundantly patched on separate access switches.
An endpoint that requires 1 Gbps or less is connected to a Nexus 2000 switch. It is absolutely recommended to dual home the server by connecting it to the network by at least 2 cables. The network must be configured in an active/passive manner.
An endpoint that requires 10 Gbps or more is connected to a Nexus 5000 switch. In every other aspect, it’s the same as an endpoint that is connected at 1 Gbps. As a standard, the server shall be connected to the network by at least 2 cables. The network shall be configured in an active/passive manner.
Managed LAN services is Cegeka managed service which consist of the monitoring, management, maintenance, supervision, administration and planning of all functions relating to infrastructure and components of local LAN network.
The Managed LAN service releases <klant> from the day-to-day management of the LAN infrastructure. Cegeka manages the LAN network on a remote (i.e. off-site) basis from our operation center, proactively fixing faults and keeping the service running. Cegeka also provides reports on the status of your network and informs <klant> about incidents that occur.
The Cegeka offering includes Site-to-Site connectivity for customers or third parties towards the customer networks in the datacenter based on IPSec connections over the Internet (or when required, over WAN connections). Depending on the type of VPN tunnel and the number of tunnels required, Cegeka will either host the tunnel end-point on the customer dedicated virtual firewall instance or on separate VPN end-point hardware.
Both Policy Based and Route Based VPNs are supported. Note that with route based VPNs, it is possible to create high-available VPN connections (by means of secondary VPN connections that can be automatically failed over to in case of primary connection failure) and also to use these as backup to a primary (routed) WAN connection.
Managed Wireless LAN (AccesPoint) services is Cegeka managed service which consist of the monitoring, management, maintenance, supervision, administration and planning of all functions relating to infrastructure and components of Wireless LAN.
The Cegeka datacenter connectivity service interconnects the datacenters. The Service is built using redundant Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing (DWDM) devices. Eight Ethernet connections are configured on these devices. The interconnect lines are divided and managed by two separate Telco-providers ensuring the highest levels of redundancy. The four DWDM paths have a 100% geographical separated track. These paths have no components in common. The Ethernet and fiber channel connections have their own dedicated fibers. Both LAN and SAN have a dedicated bandwidth of 8 Gbps.
In view of maximum redundancy and availability, all eight links are joined together into a multi-chassis Ethernet channel. In case of failure on one or more connections, the data traffic will be switched over immediately to one of the remaining connections.
The latency between the two datacenters is low. A roundtrip time of 2 to 4ms on the Ethernet connection and a maximum of 2ms on the fiber channel.
The load balancing is performed based on Cegeka’s load balancing managed service. The setup consists of redundant F5 LTM controllers. The load balancing is capable of load balancing internal and external applications.
Managed dedicated Load Balancer services is Cegeka managed service which consist of the monitoring, management, maintenance, supervision, administration and planning of all functions relating to the infrastructure and components of the load balancer environment,
Cegeka manages the loadbalancer components on a remote (i.e. off-site) basis from our operation center, proactively fixing faults and keeping the service running. Cegeka also provide reports on the status of the loadbalancer infrastructure and informs <klant> about incidents that occur.

Cegeka leverages IaC (Infrastructure as Code) not only to deploy resources but also to keep them compliant with their configuration template during their lifecycle. IaC does not only increase the efficiency of initial resource deployment but also improves later configuration updates. Cegeka conducts configuration changes by changing the configuration template instead of changing directly the resource.
This approach provides multiple benefits: configuration templates are versioned so changes can be later audited, configuration templates also provide a global view on the entire configuration providing better insight about potential change impact.

It identifies all resources on a network and makes them accessible to users and applications. Resources include e-mail addresses, computers and peripheral devices such as printers. Microsoft Exchange is our proposed solution for the messaging services. The messaging platform is integrated with the Active Directory, which provides a central administration model for the users.
All Exchange users will have their Outlook client setup to operate in ‘cached mode’. When users first log on to their mailbox, a copy of their mailbox is downloaded to the client creating an offline store (‘ost’) file. If you work offline, whether by choice or due to a connection problem, your data is still available to you wherever you are.
The Calendar function is a standard feature of Exchange. In collaboration with Outlook, users are able to schedule their free / busy time and share that information with other Exchange users. Resource mailboxes can be assigned for conference room booking with Exchange. A user locates the conference room name from the Global Address List within his / her Outlook client and schedules a meeting time against that room. These resource mailboxes are shared for all users giving any user the ability to see room availability.

Cegeka takes full responsibility for the technical application landscape. Cegeka shall be responsible in providing the following activities including all dependency components needed to have a business application up and running, i.e. IIS, J2EE or similar components (i.e. middleware or related applications).
TAM service desk support
Incidents and requests about the business applications are registered by Cegeka service desk agents. If possible, Cegeka service desk handles the incident or request using standardized procedures. Otherwise, it is transferred to second line engineers, who have a thorough knowledge about the application.
Incidents shall be registered, based on customer calls or on events generated by Cegeka’s technical monitoring of the application status and availability.
Incidents and requests are handled within the contractually agreed Service Levels. Actions in response to customer end-user calls and alerts originating from the Cegeka monitoring services are started. Tickets, which can be handled using standard procedures, are solved. The remaining tickets are handed over to engineering support.
Recurring maintenance
Most business applications require that a number of well-defined tasks are executed on a regular basis in order to ensure the availability of the business application and the system resources it uses. Examples include log rotation and connection pool clean-up. This Service ensures that the necessary tasks are executed at appropriate intervals.
Predictable incidents are avoided. Recurring maintenance activities are executed in accordance with the maintenance plan.
Engineering support
Incidents and requests that cannot be handled by Cegeka service desk by applying standardized procedures are
transferred to second line engineers. These engineers have application-specific knowledge necessary to handle these incidents with the goal of resolving them within the Service Levels as specified by their priority.
The service activity ensures the application’s technical availability is restored as per the Service Level Agreement in the event of technical failure. This doesn’t provide any guarantees on the level of functional availability and is merely allowing the functional application owner to operate and/or troubleshoot the functional application service it hosts.
In case of structural incidents, a problem ticket is created. In case an incident cannot be solved, but a work- around is available, a problem ticket is created in order to realize a structural resolution for the incident. As problems possibly lead to structural Changes in the application or infrastructure, the investigation into the problems’ cause is not in scope of this Service. This is either handled through the advanced Service or by a third party.
Conditions
Business application maintenance contracts must be in place for those activities outside the scope of Cegeka,
including third line support on specific Business Applications, and development/coding of business applications
Application patch deployment
The service activity ensures patches are deployed onto the technical application as per the predefined patch management plan or upon explicit request of customer. customer is accountable for both timings and instructions (responsibility for instructions can reside with the product vendor). By default critical (security) patches and patch sets are included in the patch management plan.
Deployment is limited to applying the patches to the technical application as is, excluding configuration changes and activation of functionality added by the patch(es) or patch set(s). The latter is done via RFC containing content, scope and exceptions. This RFC acts as patch deployment plan.
Customer remains in the lead by determining either the scope and high level planning or by explicitly requesting one off patch deployments unless patch planning & management is handled by Cegeka as part of the advanced service package.
By default, Cegeka performs two patch deployment rounds per year. Within such a patch deployment round, deployments are phased over the different environments (e.g. acceptance is patched before production). Systems within an environments are patched in bulk (e.g. all production systems).
Technical monitoring
Applications make use of various system resources, such as memory and CPU. These resources assigned need to be balanced in order to allow all applications on the system to work correctly. In this service, the resource assignment to a business application is measured in order to ensure that the resource utilization stays within a predefined range.
In case a business application requires a number of jobs to be executed automatically, this Service also ensures that the jobs are executed and that incidents are handled that occur if one of the jobs cannot be executed. This Service only concerns jobs that are executed by the application itself. In case the jobs are handled by a job
scheduler, the job execution is part of the management of the job scheduler.
Problem handling
In case a problem is created for one or more incidents, this Service investigates how the incident(s) can be solved in a structural manner and can be avoided in the future. The solution will be implemented through the RFC process.
Only problems that concern technical issues are in scope of this service. In case of functional issues, the problem must be investigated by functional application managers.
Backup planning & management
Responsibility for planning, realization and impact analysis for application backup lies with Cegeka. The backup planning & management service ensures the backup plan is defined and maintained,
whereas backup job execution – part of technical application management basic services - focuses on correct execution of backups in accordance with the backup plan and SLA. The goal is to ensure that backups are created to meet the backup requirements in the most optimal way.
Whereas the initial creation of the backup plan is prior to daily operation and thus part of transition, application landscapes change (growth, changing service level requirements, changed features). This provides opportunities to creating backups more efficiently, while still meeting customer’s functional service level requirements.
Servers are backed-up to match the functional backup requirements in the most optimal and efficient way.
Application patch planning & management
Similar to backup job execution and backup planning & management, patch planning & management is the intelligent service of critically reviewing available patches, determining relevance and possible impact within the specific customer context resulting in a more selective patch deployment, rather than predefined bulk deployment. The benefit of this service is that customer is provided with a relevant subset of available patches
reducing downtime and adverse effects on the application’s operation.
Although this Service provides analysis and guidance at the level of patches, it remains the responsibility of customer to decide upon actual deployment and timings.
Job management
This Services deals with the addition of new jobs and maintenance of existing jobs in the internal application scheduler, based upon the content and scheduling requirements, provided by the functional application manager.
The technical application managers ensure that the new job does not conflict with existing jobs and is scheduled in the most optimal way.
Optionally, in order to optimize the job schedule, the execution of individual jobs can be monitored and start times can be adjusted. This way, additional load on the system (e.g. if the number of transactions increases) can be compensated.
Functional monitoring
The Service implements and executes a combination of distinct parameter checks and pre-defined functional scenarios that are indicative of the application health. The parameters and scenarios are defined in collaboration between Cegeka and customer and are by default limited to the possibilities of the standard built-in tooling and Cegeka’s default monitoring platform. Although the management of specialized monitoring tools is not in scope of this Service, they can be used if made available separately.
The results are, dependent on the application, typically reported in happy pages or dashboards. Incidents will be acted upon through the scripted execution of standard procedures or via an escalation to second line support.
The functional availability of the application is monitored and disruptions of the functional availability are acted upon in a timely manner according to SLA of incident management. When functional checks are violated the necessary actions are initiated via service desk and/or engineering support the Cegeka Customer Care Portal can be used to publish the monitoring status information unless application specific tooling is used.
The below Services can be contracted separately per application:
Application Hardening
Application hardening is the service activity pursuing the application’s security configuration meets business requirements. As a first step, the security configuration parameters are screened, the optimal settings are determined and documented, based upon public guidelines and standards. Upon deployment the hardened configuration is applied to the application and maintained during its further operational life cycle.
Together with the hardening process, possible exceptions are well documented to keep trace of deviations from the original/standard, both at the level of content and approval(s).
Using the configuration compliance scanning service, the hardening of the systems will be checked and guaranteed.
Performance monitoring
In this Service, the performance of the application is assessed using built-in functionality or vendor provided tools. The metrics measured include (but are not limited to), depending on the type of application, response times and resource usage.

The shared managed Internet connectivity service has no limitation on bandwidth used by <klant>’s, apart from the total available physical capacity. The usage is monitored and evaluated. The internet access consists of multiple Gbps internet connections. As soon as there is a not enough bandwidth available for the shared service, the bandwidth is upgraded.
The consumed bandwidth of <klant> will be monitored and invoiced based on the PxQ model where only the 95th percentile is taken into account on monthly basis. The bandwidth usage during a month is measured each 5 minutes. All these samples are ordered and the top 5 percent of measurements is discarded (to avoid charging for brief peak usage). The mark that is then the highest is used as the reference for the Internet usage charge. By discarding the top 5% there is on average about 30 to 36 hours a month of peak/burst usage possible without being charged for that.
Cegeka does not cap the bandwidth, to avoid incidents with respect to bandwidth shortage. Cegeka has more than sufficient bandwidth available to sustain the load of <klant>. A strict capacity management process is in place consisting of alerting on above 60% usage of our Internet bandwidth and additionally manually checked by an engineer each week. When we hit the 60% mark, an upgrade of capacity will implemented, to ensure sufficient capacity for the <klant>.
<klant> can bring its own Public IP addresses to the Cegeka cloud. As part of the migration the public IP addresses will be made available and announced that this IP address range is now available in the Cegeka datacenter.
Cegeka can provide public IP addresses to <klant>. This is an option in case the public IP addresses are owned by the current provider and cannot be migrated to the Cegeka datacenter.
Cegeka uses Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) for the internet access with own public IP addresses which can easily move from one to another Internet provider. The Cegeka set-up consists of 2x2 redundant BGP routers to separate ISP providers.
The Cegeka datacenters have their own AS (Autonomous System) number. In order to have an AS-number, a service provider needs to comply with many requirements. One of the requirements is that the connections are dual-homed with two independent internet providers.
Standard features
The Internet connectivity service manages and administrates the components of the Internet connectivity service infrastructure in the Cegeka datacenters. These components are:
The Cegeka Shared Internet connection has a dual layer protection against DDoS attacks. In the Cegeka datacenters there are inline anti-DDoS protection devices that can protect against non-volumetric DDoS attacks (e.g. attacks based on protocol level, like ACK attacks) and that block these attacks typically within 18 seconds or less. For volumetric attacks, Cegeka uses a scrubbing street. As this requires a BGP rerouting towards the scrubbing street, the time for this to take effect is slightly higher. Cegeka can re-route certain blocks of her IP addresses and only reroutes blocks that are under attack. The rerouted attack traffic is separated from the rerouted valid traffic by the scrubbing street and only the valid traffic is then provided to Cegeka. Due to the shared nature of the Internet connection, other customers under attack may influence <klant> and vice versa. In practice this seldom leads to issues and due to the use of the scrubbing street any impact is only short-lived. For reference, in the past two years Cegeka only activated the scrubbing street pro-actively (before any actual DDoS attack, based on market information and threat intel)
When <klant> is under DDoS attack for more than 15 minutes, the scrubbing street will be activated.
In case of security incidents, Client will be notified according to the agreed incident management procedures.

With more and more companies gearing up to meet the fast-paced challenges of the business world, the need for IMACD services is growing. The Cegeka IMACD services offer long and short-term IMACD solutions for a wide range of organizational needs.
Our services complement our workplace outsourcing services by providing skilled professionals, with extensive knowledge, unique skill sets and the ability to handle peak workloads; thus allowing Customers to concentrate on core business areas. We offer immediate access to our trained staff and the tools to support both your non-routine and unique needs, to ensure business continuity. break & fix:
The Cegeka Break & Fix services (onsite service) are available in two standard flavours: basic and premium.
Cegeka basic onsite services will establish the necessary service management structures with local staff to be able to act according to agreed SLAs in case of hardware failure. In this case hardware for spare parts is owned by the Customer’s organization and the availability on site is also ensured by the Customer. To assist in this preparation, Cegeka of course is able to plan and organize all necessary tasks together with the Customer to prepare the environment for SLA-based onsite operation (optional).
Cegeka premium onsite services will establish the necessary service management structures with local staff including the necessary spare part handling to be able to act according to agreed SLAs. In this case hardware for spare parts is owned by the Customer’s organization but the local availability as well as care pack repair in case of defects and refill of spare stocks, tracking & reporting is managed by Cegeka as part of the service.
The standard SLA is ‘end of next business day’ and will ensure that incidents are fixed by the end of the next local business day at the latest.
It is possible to agree on specific SLAs, spare policies and local tasks as part of Cegeka’s individual onsite service.
security operations center services:
The SOC services are delivered by the Cegeka SOC and include the operational day-to-day activities needed to detect and respond to security incidents. The aim of the SOC is to:
Security incident management
Cegeka monitors the alarms generated by the SOAP and uses this platform for “hunting” undiscovered alerts. Next to security incident discovery, the aim of hunting is to detect new opportunities for the production or modification of scenarios.
The output of the SOAP includes system outputs, trend analysis, anomalies and Cegeka threat intelligence. After analysis, the output is declared as a security incident, classified as Priority 1, 2, 3 or 4 and communicated to the appropriate resolution group according to an agreed communication plan. Depending on the priority of the incident, this can be by email or phone or any other communication channel and will be based on (an) existing escalation process(es) as much as possible. Cegeka provides a recommended remediation, and assists the resolution team in charge with performing root cause and impact analysis and managing the status of the security incident ticket. The problem management process is used to investigate the lessons learned and propose additional measures. The change management process is used to implement the additional measures.

rbac:
RBAC (Role Based Access Control) is the name given to the process regulating the permissions granted to users with regards to Azure resources. These permissions define the actions that a user is allowed to perform (ex: read only, configuration modification, switch on/off,…) on Azure resources. These permissions are resource specific so a user can have reader rights on a set of resource, owner rights on other resources and no right on the rest.
The RBAC management activity consists in continuously reviewing the state of these permissions, providing recommendations and modifying these permissions upon request.
governance and organization:
Over time, as consumption scales, Azure resources tend to proliferate and could become hardly manageable without appropriate governance and organization.
The Cegeka resource/subscription management service ensures that Azure resources remain organized based on a pre-defined structure, for instance based on use case, delegation model or customer internal organization. This service also simplifies other daily operations by keeping resources conform with a predefined tagging and naming convention. Finally it ensures further scalability by checking that sufficient room remains compared to Azure limits and initiating pro-active actions when limits are getting close.
security best practices: The base service includes the management of globally applicable security mechanisms based on global tools (individual security components or configurations are covered separately). Essentially the service focusses on managing security tools like Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Azure DDoS and Azure advisor as well as ensuring that Cegeka and Microsoft security best practices are applied.
networking: The VNet service includes the management of Azure networking resources providing basic connectivity inside Azure. Components providing advanced functionalities (LB, Application gateway,…) or connectivity outside of Azure are covered separately.
service health:
Service health provides a personalized view of the health of the Azure services and regions you're using. This is the best place to look for service impacting communications about outages, planned maintenance activities, and other health advisories because the authenticated Service Health experience knows which services and resources you currently use.
The management and resolution of backbone level incidents is included in the base service package. Backbone incidents include incidents not related to an individual resource like zonal/regional unavailability.
billing: Cegeka provides billing services for the Azure consumption of CSP customers. This service is included inside the base service package. This doesn’t mean that the base service price includes Azure charges but that these Azure charges can be billed by Cegeka, jointly with other Cegeka services.
integrations:
This service includes the integration into the Cegeka support platforms. This is needed to support and manage the environments with our engineers and architects, in an efficient and secure way.
You also get access to our incident management portal to allow incident creation and follow-up on support tickets.
compliance:
Azure offers a lot of new possibilities. The breed of possibilities may introduce a challenge to keep resources compliant without reducing choice freedom.
Cegeka helps solving this issue by decoupling compliancy from design. The compliancy service consists in managing a predefined set of compliancy rules and to leverage Azure policy to continuously monitor the compliancy state of deployed resources with the predefined rules.
cost management: As Azure environments scale, it remains important to keep consumption under control, traceable and manageable. Azure environments are typically used to host applications from different company departments and cost centers. Cegeka cost management leverages different Azure tools to help customers understand their consumption, keep it under desired limits and enable cost allocation, chargeback or showback strategies.
cost optimization:
Azure offers multiple ways to consume its resources and the charges also generally depend from the use duration or sizes of selected resources. Consequently, it is important to keep azure consumption aligned with real needs.
The cost optimization service of Cegeka is meant to optimize the consumption by continuously scanning deployed resources, providing optimization recommendations and reduce charges.
microsoft azure individual components: Besides the base service Cegeka delivers several other services for Microsoft Azure. Typically these services matches the services delivered by Microsoft. Cegeka performs additional services in this regard.
azure vms:
Azure virtual machines like traditional VM’s provide full control on inner content (operating system, application & configuration) to administrators. However, while they do not compare with platform features built in other PaaS offerings, Azure VM’s offer more features than traditional VM’s and Cegeka can manage these features making Azure VM’s more cloud native.
A prerequisite for this add on is that an existing Disaster Recovery Plan is in place.
Some features are covered by others services
azure bastion: Azure Bastion provides secure and seamless RDP/SSH connectivity to all virtual machines in a VNet, directly in the Azure portal over TLS. With Azure Bastion, virtual machines do not need a public IP address and their RDP/SSH ports is not exposed to the outside world. Azure Bastion provides access to the virtual machine directly from the Azure portal without additional client, agent, or piece of software.
aks (azure kubernetes service): While containers exist since a long time, they gained more substantial adoption over the last years. During this period of wider adoption, different container orchestration platforms co-existed for a while but Kubernetes reached the highest consensus and is now considered as the standard container orchestration platform, among other thanks to its ability to preserve container portability across on-premises and multi-cloud environments (in contrast to other proprietary solutions).
app service: Azure App service allows users to easily deploy, build and scale web apps created with .NET, .NET Core, Node.JS, Java, PHP, Ruby or Python, in containers or running on any operating system.
azure cache for redis:
Azure Cache for Redis is a fully managed, in-memory cache that enables high-performance and scalable architectures. Use it to create cloud or hybrid deployments that handle millions of requests per second at sub-millisecond latency – all with the configuration, security and availability benefits of a managed service.
While most databases store data on slower, disk-based storage, Azure Cache for Redis stores data in memory. Since memory is significantly faster than disk storage, data can be written and retrieved much more quickly.
Adding Azure Cache for Redis can increase data throughput by over 800 percent while increasing latency performance by over 1,000 percent at a cost-effective price. It’s a great way to efficiently scale applications and improve user experience without the expense of rearchitecting your underlying database.
logic app: Like Azure Function, Logic App also helps developers to build modern application architecture. Logic App provides  an orchestration platform to build automated workflows. Logic App provides extensive built-in connectors with third-party (Salesforce, SAP, Oracle, Twitter,…) or Microsoft (O365, Dynamics, BizTalk, Azure,…) services for simple enterprise integration services. It is not only serverless but also requires no code at all. Cegeka can help developers to adopt this kind of patterns by offloading non application-level activities, allowing developers to focus on the business and application logic.
azure function: Azure Function allows modern application architectures to be created, including patterns like event-based processing, microservices, LowCode (leveraging built-in features & integration), serverless, auto-scaling, cloud native (platform integration)… Cegeka can help developers to adopt this kind of patterns by offloading them from non application-level activities, allowing them to focus on the business and application logic.
azure files: Azure files allows organizations to host SMB or NFS file shares in the cloud without managing server infrastructure.
azure queues: Azure Queue Storage provides storage for large numbers of messages. Messages can be accessed from anywhere in the world via authenticated calls using HTTP or HTTPS and queues can scale very high (millions of messages). Messaging services are used to decouple applications, providing scalability based on asynchronous processing. Azure Queues is a simple messaging service providing very high scalability and complementing more advanced messaging solutions of Azure like Event Grid & Service Bus.
azure tables: Azure Table storage provides structured NoSQL data storage, providing a key/value store with a schema-less design. Because Table storage is schema-less, it's easy to adapt data structure as application needs evolve. While Azure Table provides a simple and cost efficient NoSQL table storage, it could be at term be replaced by Azure Cosmos DB Table API.
azure sql:
Azure SQL provides a PaaS platform to host SQL workloads. It provides different form factors with different customization and compliancy levels with on-premises workloads, including SQL managed instances, SQL databases & elastic pools.
The service described below focusses on managing the functionalities provided by Azure SQL allowing to host SQL workloads, it does not include pure database management.
azure database for mysql:
Azure Database for MySQL provides a PaaS platform to host MySQL workloads. It provides multiple tiers and consumption model like reservations or PAYG.
The service described below focusses on managing the functionalities provided by Azure to support and integrate Azure Database for MySQL and to keep it safe and secure, it does not include pure data management tasks.
azure database for postgresql:
Azure Database for PostgreSQL provides a PaaS platform to host PostgreSQL workloads. It provides multiple tiers and consumption model like reservations or PAYG.
The service described below focusses on managing the functionalities provided by Azure to support and integrate Azure Database for PostgreSQL and to keep it safe and secure, it does not include pure data management tasks.
azure database for mariadb:
Azure Database for MariaDB provides a PaaS platform to host MariaDB workloads. It provides multiple tiers and consumption model like reservations or PAYG.
The service described below focusses on managing the functionalities provided by Azure to support and integrate Azure Database for MariaDB and to keep it safe and secure, it does not include pure data management tasks.
azure container registry: Azure container registry provides storage and management for container images. It integrates closely with different other Azure services like AKS, App Service and Azure pipelines but can also be used with third party products like Docker Swarm, DC/OS,…
azure key vault:
Azure Key Vault provides a central location to securely store keys, passwords, certificates, and other secrets, eliminating unsafe practices like storing credentials inside application code, blob storage or developer files,…
Azure Key Vault features multiple layers of redundancy to make sure that keys and secrets remain available to application even if individual components of the service fail.
azure site recovery: Azure Site Recovery (ASR) provides disaster recovery service, typically between two Azure regions but it can also recover on-premises VMs to Azure or to another on-premises datacenter. ASR restores VMs based on recovery points which can be of two types: crash-consistent and app-consistent recovery points. Crash-consistent recovery points only include disk data while app-consistent ones also include memory data flushed by the application at snapshot time. If desired, ASR can also automatically deploy the target environment (subscription, resource group, vnet, storage account, replica managed disks, availability zones & availability sets). Target VMs are only created when a failover is initiated (avoiding useless charges). By default, the failover occurs manually (single click/cli command) but could be scripted if trigger conditions can be clearly identified.
azure dns:
Azure DNS is a hosting service for DNS domains that provides name resolution by using Microsoft Azure infrastructure. By hosting your domains in Azure, you can manage your DNS records by using the same credentials, APIs, tools, and billing as your other Azure services.
Azure DNS consists in a global network of DNS name server allowing Azure to offer a 100% SLA on DNS request resolution. Azure DNS can host private and public domains. In the case of private domains, Azure DNS also provides auto-registration of Azure VMs located in a VNet (auto-registration applies to a single VNet).
azure s2s vpn connection: An Azure site-to-site VPN connection is a virtual private network (VPN) connection between an on-premises network and an Azure Virtual Network (VNet). This connection allows traffic to flow securely between the on-premises network and the VNet in Azure over the internet.
azure expressroute (optional): Azure ExpressRoute (ER) provides a dedicated connection between Microsoft cloud (including Azure and O365 services) and a telecommunication provider extending the connectivity to a final point, typically a customer location. An ExpressRoute is an alternative to Internet providing dedicated bandwidth and private connectivity (non encrypted by default). It can be combined with a VPN gateway or another ExpressRoute to provide high-availability.
azure cdn: Azure CDN provides content delivery acceleration to end users thanks to Azure worldwide network and by leveraging the closest POP to serve user requests. Azure CDN accelerates both static and dynamic content delivery. Static content can be cached and be subsequently served locally from the closest PoP (offloading also central back-end resources). Dynamic content delivery can also be accelerated by leveraging Microsoft backbone from the closest PoP to the backend resource (instead of the Internet backbone) and by leveraging smart routing optimizations.
azure load balancer:
An Azure Load Balancer improves application performance and high-availability by distributing client requests between a set of equivalent servers and by continuously checking their health. An Azure Load Balancer helps applications to scale horizontally and remain available in case of local failure or outage impacting an entire availability zone.
Moreover, Load Balancer belongs to the rich suite of Azure load-balancing solutions and is specially meant for L4 load-balancing in a single region, other solutions are available for single region L7 load-balancing (Application gateway) and for multi-region HTTP(S)/DNS-based load-balancing (respectively Front Door and Traffic Manager).
azure application gateway:
An Azure Application Gateway provides the same application performance, scalability and high-availability as a Load Balancer but it also provides additional benefits by its ability to process L7 (HTTP/HTTPS) attributes. An application Gateway is able to make optimal routing decisions based on HTTP(S) URI, headers or query strings. It is also able to terminate TLS sessions, providing data encryption in transit. It is also able to make deeper evaluation of the health of backend resources, including their ability to properly answer HTTP or custom probes. Finally an Application Gateway can optionally be used as a WAF or as an ingress for AKS.
Moreover, Application Gateway belongs to the rich suite of Azure load-balancing solutions and is specially meant for L7 load-balancing in a single region, other solutions are available for single region L4 load-balancing (Load Balancer) and for multi-region HTTP(S)/DNS-based load-balancing (respectively Front Door and Traffic Manager).
azure traffic manager:
Azure Traffic Manager is a DNS-based traffic load balancer. This service allows you to distribute traffic to your public facing applications across the global Azure regions. Traffic Manager also provides your public endpoints with high availability and quick responsiveness.
Traffic Manager uses DNS to direct the client requests to the appropriate service endpoint based on a traffic-routing method. Traffic manager also provides health monitoring for every endpoint. The endpoint can be any Internet-facing service hosted inside or outside of Azure. Traffic Manager provides a range of traffic-routing methods and endpoint monitoring options to suit different application needs and automatic failover models. Traffic Manager is resilient to failure, including the failure of an entire Azure region.
azure firewall: Azure Firewall complements the network filtering function provided by Network Security Groups (NSGs) by adding a few additional benefits including: central management, fully stateful firewall, NAT, FQDN filtering, Threat-intelligence filtering (based on malicious IP and domains). Compared to third-party solutions, it also provides native high-availability and unrestricted scalability.
azure service bus:
Service Bus provides message brokering services allowing application components to be decoupled and still communicate efficiently and whatever their location, in Azure or on-premises. It supports modern application architecture by enabling asynchronous messaging, transaction processing (with ordered delivery) and consumer load-balancing.
Cegeka can help developers to adopt this kind of patterns by offloading them from non application-level activities, allowing them to focus on the business and application logic.
azure analysis service: Built on SQL Server Analysis Service (SSAS), Azure Analysis Service is a serverless and cloud native data modelling service to analyse and combine data from multiple sources.
azure databricks: Azure Databricks is a cloud native deployment of Spark based analytics
azure data factory: Built from SQL SSIS, Azure Data Factory is a cloud native service to build & run data integration pipelines, including ETL or ELT processing. This service allows raw big data from multiple sources to be cleaned and centralised for further processing or analysis.
azure data lake storage: Data Lake Storage builds on blob storage and extends its native features and benefits (cheap, massively scalable and durable storage) to big data analytics use cases thanks to additional features like file system semantics and file-level security.
azure machine learning: Azure machine learning allows a tenant to create a model in Azure Machine Learning or use an open-source model and run them in Azure.
azure purview: Azure Purview provides a holistic overview and governance service of a tenant data landscape, whatever the location of the data, including Azure, other clouds, on-premises and SaaS data. Governance services include among other data discovery, classification and lineage in general.
azure synapse analytics: Formerly known as SQL data warehouse, Azure Synapse Analytics provides a complete big data analysis and integration platform. This includes data warehouse storage (based on Azure Data Lake), SQL & T-SQL distributed query service (including support for streaming data and machine learning models), Spark big data engine, ETL/ELT integration pipelines (similar to Azure data factory pipelines)
api management:
API management provides a central point to expose APIs securely and at scale. It allows application owner to define API consumption policies like quota & throttling. It also simplifies API consumption by means of a developer portal for consumer onboarding (API documentation, interactive console, account creation, quota visibility,…) & productization. Finally it also provides mechanisms to control their lifecycle, including updates & visibility.
Cegeka can help developers to adopt this kind of patterns by offloading them from non application-level activities, allowing them to focus on the API logic.
remote workplace services (citrix): The Remote workplace services is the service that manages the Central workspace infrastructure, a virtual workspace located at Cegeka’s datacenters and remotely available for end-users. This service manages the following topics:
